parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 1–13
parts.passageTitle: The development of the London underground railway

passage.text: The development of the London underground railway

In the first half of the 1800s, London’s population grew at an astonishing rate, and the central area became increasingly congested. In addition, the expansion of the overground railway network resulted in more and more passengers arriving in the capital. However, in 1846, a Royal Commission decided that the railways should not be allowed to enter the City, the capital’s historic and business centre. The result was that the overground railway stations formed a ring around the City. The area within consisted of poorly built, overcrowded slums and the streets were full of horse-drawn traffic. Crossing the City became a nightmare. It could take an hour and a half to travel 8 km by horse-drawn carriage or bus. Numerous schemes were proposed to resolve these problems, but few succeeded.

Amongst the most vocal advocates for a solution to London’s traffic problems was Charles Pearson, who worked as a solicitor for the City of London. He saw both social and economic advantages in building an underground railway that would link the overground railway stations together and clear London slums at the same time. His idea was to relocate the poor workers who lived in the inner-city slums to newly constructed suburbs, and to provide cheap rail travel for them to get to work. Pearson’s ideas gained support amongst some businessmen and in 1851 he submitted a plan to Parliament. It was rejected, but coincided with a proposal from another group for an underground connecting line, which Parliament passed.

The two groups merged and established the Metropolitan Railway Company in August 1854. The company’s plan was to construct an underground railway line from the Great Western Railway’s (GWR) station at Paddington to the edge of the City at Farringdon Street – a distance of almost 5 km. The organisation had difficulty in raising the funding for such a radical and expensive scheme, not least because of the critical articles printed by the press. Objectors argued that the tunnels would collapse under the weight of traffic overhead, buildings would be shaken and passengers would be poisoned by the emissions from the train engines. However, Pearson and his partners persisted.

The GWR, aware that the new line would finally enable them to run trains into the heart of the City, invested almost £250,000 in the scheme. Eventually, over a five-year period, £1m was raised. The chosen route ran beneath existing main roads to minimise the expense of demolishing buildings. Originally scheduled to be completed in 21 months, the construction of the underground line took three years. It was built just below street level using a technique known as ‘cut and cover’. A trench about ten metres wide and six metres deep was dug, and the sides temporarily help up with timber beams. Brick walls were then constructed, and finally a brick arch was added to create a tunnel. A two-metre-deep layer of soil was laid on top of the tunnel and the road above rebuilt.

The Metropolitan line, which opened on 10 January 1863, was the world’s first underground railway. On its first day, almost 40,000 passengers were carried between Paddington and Farringdon, the journey taking about 18 minutes. By the end of the Metropolitan’s first year of operation, 9. 5 million journeys had been made.

Even as the Metropolitan began operation, the first extensions to the line were being authorised; these were built over the next five years, reaching Moorgate in the east to London and Hammersmith in the west. The original plan was to pull the trains with steam locomotives, using firebricks in the boilers to provide steam, but these engines were never introduced. Instead, the line used specially designed locomotives that were fitted with water tanks in which steam could be condensed. However, smoke and fumes remained a problem, even though ventilation shafts were added to the tunnels.

Despite the extension of the underground railway, by the 1880s, congestion on London’s streets had become worse. The problem was partly that the existing underground lines formed a circuit around the centre of London and extended to the suburbs, but did not cross the capital’s centre. The ‘cut and cover’ method of construction was not an option in this part of the capital. The only alternative was to tunnel deep underground.

Although the technology to create these tunnels existed, steam locomotives could not be used in such a confined space. It wasn’t until the development of a reliable electric motor, and a means of transferring power from the generator to a moving train, that the world’s first deep-level electric railway, the City & South London Railway, became possible. The line opened in 1890, and ran from the City to Stockwell, south of the River Thames. The trains were made up of three carriages and driven by electric engines. The carriages were narrow and had tiny windows just below the roof because it was thought that passengers would not want to look out at the tunnel walls. The line was not without its problems, mainly caused by an unreliable power supply, Although the City & South London Railway was a great technical achievement, it did not make a profit. Then, in 1900, the Central London Railway, known as the ‘Tuppenny Tube’, began operation using new electric locomotives. It was very popular and soon afterwards new railways and extensions were added to the growing tube network. By 1907, the heart of today’s Underground system was in place.

questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 1-6

Complete the notes below.

Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.
questionGroups.instruction: Questions 1-6Complete the notes below.Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.
questionGroups.blockHtml:
The London underground railway

The problem

  • The 1_______ of London increased rapidly between 1800 and 1850
  • The streets were full of horse-drawn vehicles

The proposed solution

  • Charles Pearson, a solicitor, suggested building an underground railway
  • Building the railway would make it possible to move people to better housing in the 2_______
  • A number of 3_______ agreed with Pearson’s idea
  • The company initially had problems getting the 4_______ needed for the project
  • Negative articles about the project appeared in the 5_______

The construction

  • The chosen route did not require many buildings to be pulled down
  • The ‘cut and cover’ method was used to construct the tunnels
  • With the completion of the brick arch, the tunnel was covered with 6_______
questionGroups.noteContent:
**The problem**
 • The [1] of London increased rapidly between 1800 and 1850
 • The streets were full of horse-drawn vehicles
**The proposed solution**
 • Charles Pearson, a solicitor, suggested building an underground railway
 • Building the railway would make it possible to move people to better housing in the [2]
 • A number of [3] agreed with Pearson’s idea
 • The company initially had problems getting the [4] needed for the project
 • Negative articles about the project appeared in the [5]
**The construction**
 • The chosen route did not require many buildings to be pulled down
 • The ‘cut and cover’ method was used to construct the tunnels
 • With the completion of the brick arch, the tunnel was covered with [6]

questions.prompt: The 1 …………………… of London increased rapidly between 1800 and 1850
questions.explanation:
ước 1: Keyword:
“increased rapidly between 1800 and 1850”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 1: “In the first half of the 1800s, London’s population grew at an astonishing rate...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“grew at an astonishing rate” = increased rapidly
Danh từ cần điền là thứ đã tăng nhanh → population
=> Đáp án: population
questions.questionHtml:
The London underground railway

The problem

  • The 1_______ of London increased rapidly between 1800 and 1850
  • The streets were full of horse-drawn vehicles

The proposed solution

  • Charles Pearson, a solicitor, suggested building an underground railway
  • Building the railway would make it possible to move people to better housing in the 2_______
  • A number of 3_______ agreed with Pearson’s idea
  • The company initially had problems getting the 4_______ needed for the project
  • Negative articles about the project appeared in the 5_______

The construction

  • The chosen route did not require many buildings to be pulled down
  • The ‘cut and cover’ method was used to construct the tunnels
  • With the completion of the brick arch, the tunnel was covered with 6_______

questions.prompt: Building the railway would make it possible to move people to better housing in the 2 ……………………
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
move people to better housing
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 2: “...relocate the poor workers who lived in the inner-city slums to newly constructed suburbs...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“relocate” = move
“better housing” = newly constructed suburbs
Nơi người dân được chuyển tới là suburbs
=> Đáp án: suburbs

questions.prompt: A number of 3 …………………… agreed with Pearson’s idea
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“agreed with Pearson’s idea”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 2: “Pearson’s ideas gained support amongst some businessmen...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“gained support” = agreed with / supported
Một số businessmen đã ủng hộ ý tưởng của Pearson
=> Đáp án: businessmen

questions.prompt: The company initially had problems getting the 4 …………………… needed for the project
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“problems getting the … needed”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 3: “The organisation had difficulty in raising the funding for such a radical and expensive scheme...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“raising the funding” = getting the money needed
Danh từ phù hợp cần điền là funding
=> Đáp án: funding

questions.prompt: Negative articles about the project appeared in the 5 ……………………
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“Negative articles”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 3: “...because of the critical articles printed by the press.”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“critical articles” = negative articles
Các bài báo tiêu cực xuất hiện trên press
=> Đáp án: press

questions.prompt: With the completion of the brick arch, the tunnel was covered with 6 ……………………
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“covered with”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 4: “A two-metre-deep layer of soil was laid on top of the tunnel...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“laid on top of” = covered with
Đường hầm được phủ bằng soil
=> Đáp án: soil

questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 7-13

Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 1?

TRUE if the statement agrees with the information
FALSE if the statement contradicts the information
NOT GIVEN if there is no information on this
questionGroups.instruction: Questions 7-13Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 1?TRUE if the statement agrees with the informationFALSE if the statement contradicts the informationNOT GIVEN if there is no information on this
questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: Other countries had built underground railways before the Metropolitan line opened.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“before the Metropolitan line opened”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 5: “The Metropolitan line... was the world’s first underground railway.”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“world’s first” = tuyến tàu điện ngầm đầu tiên trên thế giới
Nghĩa là chưa có nước nào xây trước đó
Statement nói có nước khác xây trước → trái với bài
=> Đáp án: FALSE

questions.prompt: More people than predicted travelled on the Metropolitan line on the first day.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“more people than predicted”, “first day”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 5: “On its first day, almost 40,000 passengers were carried...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
Bài chỉ cho biết số lượng hành khách thực tế
Không đề cập số lượng được dự đoán trước đó
Không đủ thông tin để kết luận
=> Đáp án: NOT GIVEN

questions.prompt: The use of ventilation shafts failed to prevent pollution in the tunnels.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“ventilation shafts”, “pollution”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 6: “However, smoke and fumes remained a problem, even though ventilation shafts were added to the tunnels.”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“remained a problem” = vẫn còn là vấn đề
Dù có ventilation shafts nhưng ô nhiễm vẫn chưa được giải quyết hoàn toàn
=> Đáp án: TRUE

questions.prompt: A different approach from the ‘cut and cover’ technique was required in London’s central area.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“different approach”, “‘cut and cover’”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 7: “The ‘cut and cover’ method of construction was not an option in this part of the capital. The only alternative was to tunnel deep underground.”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“not an option” = không thể sử dụng
Vì vậy cần một phương pháp khác với cut and cover
=> Đáp án: TRUE

questions.prompt: The windows on City & South London trains were at eye level.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“windows”, “eye level”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 8: “The carriages were narrow and had tiny windows just below the roof...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“just below the roof” = ngay dưới mái tàu
Không phải ngang tầm mắt (eye level)
Statement trái với bài đọc
=> Đáp án: FALSE

questions.prompt: The City & South London Railway was a financial success.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“financial success”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 8: “...it did not make a profit.”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“did not make a profit” = không tạo ra lợi nhuận
Vì vậy tuyến đường sắt này không thành công về tài chính
=> Đáp án: FALSE

questions.prompt: Trains on the ‘Tuppenny Tube’ nearly always ran on time.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“ran on time”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 8: “It was very popular and soon afterwards new railways and extensions were added...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
Bài đọc không đề cập việc tàu có chạy đúng giờ hay không
Không đủ thông tin để xác định
=> Đáp án: NOT GIVEN

parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 14–26

passage.text: Stadiums: past, present and future

Stadiums are among the oldest forms of urban architecture: vast stadiums where the public could watch sporting events were at the centre of western city life as far back as the ancient Greek and Roman Empires, well before the construction of the great medieval cathedrals and the grand 19th- and 20th-century railway stations which dominated urban skylines in later eras.

Today, however, stadiums are regarded with growing scepticism. Construction costs can soar above £1 billion, and stadiums finished for major events such as the Olympic Games or the FIFA World Cup have notably fallen into disuse and disrepair.

But this need not be the cause. History shows that stadiums can drive urban development and adapt to the culture of every age. Even today, architects and planners are finding new ways to adapt the mono-functional sports arenas which became emblematic of modernisation during the 20th century.

The amphitheatre* of Arles Amphitheatre in southwest France, with a capacity of 25,000 spectators, is perhaps the best example of just how versatile stadiums can be. Built by the Romans in 90 AD, it became a fortress with four towers after the fifth century, and was then transformed into a village containing more than 200 houses. With the growing interest in conservation during the 19th century, it was converted back into an arena for the staging of bullfights, thereby returning the structure to its original use as a venue for public spectacles.

Another example is the imposing arena of Arena of Verona in northern Italy, with space for 30,000 spectators, which was built 60 years before the Arles amphitheatre and 40 years before Rome’s famous Colosseum. It has endured the centuries and is currently considered one of the world’s prime sites for opera, thanks to its outstanding acoustics.

The area in the centre of the Italian town of Lucca, known as the Piazza dell’ Anfiteatro, is yet another impressive example of an amphitheatre becoming absorbed into the fabric of the city. The site evolved in a similar way to Arles and was progressively filled with buildings from the Middle Ages until the 19th century, variously used as houses, a salt depot and a prison. But rather than reverting to an arena, it became a market square, designed by Romanticist architect Lorenzo Nottolini. Today, the ruins of the amphitheatre remain embedded in the various shops and residences surrounding the public square.

There are many similarities between modern stadiums and the ancient amphitheatres intended for games. But some of the flexibility was lost at the beginning of the 20th century, as stadiums were developed using new products such as steel and reinforced concrete, and made use of bright lights for night-time matches.

Many such stadiums are situated in suburban areas, designed for sporting use only and surrounded by parking lots. These factors mean that they may not be as accessible to the general public, require more energy to run and contribute to urban heat.

But many of today’s most innovative architects see scope for the stadium to help improve the city. Among the current strategies, two seem to be having particular success: the stadium as an urban hub, and as a power plant.

There’s a growing trend for stadiums to be equipped with public spaces and services that serve a function beyond sport, such as hotels, retail outlets, conference centres, restaurants and bars, children’s playgrounds and green space. Creating mixed-use developments such as this reinforces compactness and multi-functionality, making more efficient use of land and helping to regenerate urban spaces.

This opens the space up to families and a wider cross-section of society, instead of catering only to sportspeople and supporters. There have been many examples of this in the UK: the mixed-use facilities at Wembley Stadium and Old Trafford have become a blueprint for many other stadiums in the world.

The phenomenon of stadium as power stations has arisen from the idea that energy problems can be overcome by integrating interconnected buildings by means of a smart grid, which is an electricity supply network that uses digital communications technology to detect and react to local changes in usage, without significant energy losses. Stadiums are ideal for these purposes, because their canopies have a large surface area for fitting photovoltaic panels and rise high enough (more than 40 metres) to make use of micro wind turbines.

Freiburg Mage Solar Stadium in Germany is the first of a new wave of stadiums as power plants, which also includes the Amsterdam Arena and the Kaohsiung Stadium. The latter, inaugurated in 2009, has 8,844 photovoltaic panels producing up to 1. 14 GWh of electricity annually. This reduces the annual output of carbon dioxide by 660 tons and supplies up to 80 percent of the surrounding area when the stadium is not in use. This is proof that a stadium can serve its city, and have a decidedly positive impact in terms of reduction of CO2 emissions.

Sporting arenas have always been central to the life and culture of cities. In every era, the stadium has acquired new value and uses: from military fortress to residential village, public space to theatre and most recently a field for experimentation in advanced engineering. The stadium of today now brings together multiple functions, thus helping cities to create a sustainable future.

amphitheatre: (especially in Greek and Roman architecture) an open circular or oval building with a central space surrounded by tiers of seats for spectators, for the presentation of dramatic or sporting events

parts.passageHtml:
Stadiums: past, present and future

A.

Stadiums are among the oldest forms of urban architecture: vast stadiums where the public could watch sporting events were at the centre of western city life as far back as the ancient Greek and Roman Empires, well before the construction of the great medieval cathedrals and the grand 19th- and 20th-century railway stations which dominated urban skylines in later eras.

Today, however, stadiums are regarded with growing scepticism. Construction costs can soar above £1 billion, and stadiums finished for major events such as the Olympic Games or the FIFA World Cup have notably fallen into disuse and disrepair.

But this need not be the cause. History shows that stadiums can drive urban development and adapt to the culture of every age. Even today, architects and planners are finding new ways to adapt the mono-functional sports arenas which became emblematic of modernisation during the 20th century.

B.

The amphitheatre* of Arles Amphitheatre in southwest France, with a capacity of 25,000 spectators, is perhaps the best example of just how versatile stadiums can be. Built by the Romans in 90 AD, it became a fortress with four towers after the fifth century, and was then transformed into a village containing more than 200 houses. With the growing interest in conservation during the 19th century, it was converted back into an arena for the staging of bullfights, thereby returning the structure to its original use as a venue for public spectacles.

Another example is the imposing arena of Arena of Verona in northern Italy, with space for 30,000 spectators, which was built 60 years before the Arles amphitheatre and 40 years before Rome’s famous Colosseum. It has endured the centuries and is currently considered one of the world’s prime sites for opera, thanks to its outstanding acoustics.

C.

The area in the centre of the Italian town of Lucca, known as the Piazza dell’ Anfiteatro, is yet another impressive example of an amphitheatre becoming absorbed into the fabric of the city. The site evolved in a similar way to Arles and was progressively filled with buildings from the Middle Ages until the 19th century, variously used as houses, a salt depot and a prison. But rather than reverting to an arena, it became a market square, designed by Romanticist architect Lorenzo Nottolini. Today, the ruins of the amphitheatre remain embedded in the various shops and residences surrounding the public square.

D.

There are many similarities between modern stadiums and the ancient amphitheatres intended for games. But some of the flexibility was lost at the beginning of the 20th century, as stadiums were developed using new products such as steel and reinforced concrete, and made use of bright lights for night-time matches.

Many such stadiums are situated in suburban areas, designed for sporting use only and surrounded by parking lots. These factors mean that they may not be as accessible to the general public, require more energy to run and contribute to urban heat.

E.

But many of today’s most innovative architects see scope for the stadium to help improve the city. Among the current strategies, two seem to be having particular success: the stadium as an urban hub, and as a power plant.

There’s a growing trend for stadiums to be equipped with public spaces and services that serve a function beyond sport, such as hotels, retail outlets, conference centres, restaurants and bars, children’s playgrounds and green space. Creating mixed-use developments such as this reinforces compactness and multi-functionality, making more efficient use of land and helping to regenerate urban spaces.

This opens the space up to families and a wider cross-section of society, instead of catering only to sportspeople and supporters. There have been many examples of this in the UK: the mixed-use facilities at Wembley Stadium and Old Trafford have become a blueprint for many other stadiums in the world.

F.

The phenomenon of stadium as power stations has arisen from the idea that energy problems can be overcome by integrating interconnected buildings by means of a smart grid, which is an electricity supply network that uses digital communications technology to detect and react to local changes in usage, without significant energy losses. Stadiums are ideal for these purposes, because their canopies have a large surface area for fitting photovoltaic panels and rise high enough (more than 40 metres) to make use of micro wind turbines.

Freiburg Mage Solar Stadium in Germany is the first of a new wave of stadiums as power plants, which also includes the Amsterdam Arena and the Kaohsiung Stadium. The latter, inaugurated in 2009, has 8,844 photovoltaic panels producing up to 1. 14 GWh of electricity annually. This reduces the annual output of carbon dioxide by 660 tons and supplies up to 80 percent of the surrounding area when the stadium is not in use. This is proof that a stadium can serve its city, and have a decidedly positive impact in terms of reduction of CO2 emissions.

G.

Sporting arenas have always been central to the life and culture of cities. In every era, the stadium has acquired new value and uses: from military fortress to residential village, public space to theatre and most recently a field for experimentation in advanced engineering. The stadium of today now brings together multiple functions, thus helping cities to create a sustainable future.

  • amphitheatre: (especially in Greek and Roman architecture) an open circular or oval building with a central space surrounded by tiers of seats for spectators, for the presentation of dramatic or sporting events

questionGroups.instruction: Questions 14-17Which section contains the following information?
questionGroups.blockHtml:
List of options

A. Paragraph A

B. Paragraph B

C. Paragraph C

D. Paragraph D

E. Paragraph E

F. Paragraph F

G. Paragraph G

14_______  a mention of negative attitudes towards stadium building projects

15_______  figures demonstrating the environmental benefits of a certain stadium

16_______  examples of the wide range of facilities available at some new stadiums

17_______  reference to the disadvantages of the stadiums built during a certain era

questions.prompt: a mention of negative attitudes towards stadium building projects
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“negative attitudes”, “stadium building projects”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn A: “Today, however, stadiums are regarded with growing scepticism. Construction costs can soar above £1 billion...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“growing scepticism” = thái độ tiêu cực / nghi ngờ
Đoạn này nói nhiều người có cái nhìn tiêu cực về các dự án xây sân vận động
=> Đáp án: A

questions.prompt: figures demonstrating the environmental benefits of a certain stadium
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“Figures”, “environmental benefits”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn F: “...reduces the annual output of carbon dioxide by 660 tons and supplies up to 80 percent of the surrounding area...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
Có số liệu cụ thể về lượng CO2 giảm và điện năng cung cấp
→ thể hiện lợi ích môi trường của sân vận động
=> Đáp án: F

questions.prompt: examples of the wide range of facilities available at some new stadiums
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword: 
“wide range of facilities”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn E: “...hotels, retail outlets, conference centres, restaurants and bars, children’s playgrounds and green space.”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
Đoạn này liệt kê rất nhiều tiện ích trong sân vận động hiện đại
→ phù hợp với “wide range of facilities”
=> Đáp án: E

questions.prompt: reference to the disadvantages of the stadiums built during a certain era
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“disadvantages”, “stadiums built during a certain era”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn D: “Many such stadiums are situated in suburban areas... require more energy to run and contribute to urban heat.”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
Đoạn này nói về nhược điểm của các sân vận động thế kỷ 20
→ vị trí bất tiện, tốn năng lượng, gây nhiệt đô thị
=> Đáp án: D

questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 18-22

Complete the summary below. Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.
questionGroups.instruction: Questions 18-22Complete the summary below. Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.
questionGroups.blockHtml:
Roman amphitheatres

The Roman stadium of Europe have proved very versatile. The amphitheatre of Arles, for example, was converted first into a 18_______, then into a residential area and finally into an arena where spectators could watch 19_______. Meanwhile, the arena in Verona, one of the oldest Roman amphitheatres, is famous today as a venue where 20_______ is performed. The site of Lucca’s amphitheatre has also been used for many purposes over the centuries, including the storage of 21_______. It is now a market square with

22_______ and homes incorporated into the remains of the Roman amphitheatre.
questionGroups.noteContent:
The Roman stadium of Europe have proved very versatile. The amphitheatre of Arles, for example, was converted first into a [18], then into a residential area and finally into an arena where spectators could watch [19]. Meanwhile, the arena in Verona, one of the oldest Roman amphitheatres, is famous today as a venue where [20] is performed. The site of Lucca’s amphitheatre has also been used for many purposes over the centuries, including the storage of [21]. It is now a market square with 
[22] and homes incorporated into the remains of the Roman amphitheatre.

questions.prompt: The amphitheatre of Arles, for example, was converted first into a 18 ……………………
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“converted first into a …”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn B: “...it became a fortress with four towers after the fifth century...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“became a fortress” = được chuyển đổi thành pháo đài
=> Đáp án: fortress
questions.questionHtml:
Roman amphitheatres

The Roman stadium of Europe have proved very versatile. The amphitheatre of Arles, for example, was converted first into a 18_______, then into a residential area and finally into an arena where spectators could watch 19_______. Meanwhile, the arena in Verona, one of the oldest Roman amphitheatres, is famous today as a venue where 20_______ is performed. The site of Lucca’s amphitheatre has also been used for many purposes over the centuries, including the storage of 21_______. It is now a market square with

22_______ and homes incorporated into the remains of the Roman amphitheatre.

questions.prompt: then into a residential area and finally into an arena where spectators could watch 19 …………………… .
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“spectators could watch …”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn B: “...it was converted back into an arena for the staging of bullfights...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
Người xem đến để xem các trận đấu bò tót
→ danh từ cần điền là bullfights
=> Đáp án: bullfights

questions.prompt: Meanwhile, the arena in Verona, one of the oldest Roman amphitheatres, is famous today as a venue where 20 …………………… is performed.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“venue where … is performed”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn B: “...currently considered one of the world’s prime sites for opera...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
Đây là nơi biểu diễn opera
=> Đáp án: opera

questions.prompt: The site of Lucca’s amphitheatre has also been used for many purposes over the centuries, including the storage of 21 …………………… .
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“storage of …”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn C:
“...used as houses, a salt depot and a prison.”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“salt depot” = nơi chứa muối
→ storage of salt
=> Đáp án: salt

questions.prompt: It is now a market square with 22 …………………… and homes incorporated into the remains of the Roman amphitheatre.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“market square with … and homes”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn C: “...the ruins of the amphitheatre remain embedded in the various shops and residences...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“residences” = homes
“shops” là từ còn lại phù hợp
=> Đáp án: shops

questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 23-24

Choose TWO letters, A-E.

When comparing twentieth-century stadiums to ancient amphitheatres in Section D, which TWO negative features does the writer mention?

A They are less imaginatively designed.
B They are less spacious.
C They are in less convenient locations.
D They are less versatile.
E They are made of less durable materials

listOptions.label: They are in less convenient locations.
They are made of less durable materials

questionGroups.instruction: Questions 23-24Choose TWO letters, A-E.When comparing twentieth-century stadiums to ancient amphitheatres in Section D, which TWO negative features does the writer mention?A They are less imaginatively designed.B They are less spacious.C They are in less convenient locations.D They are less versatile.E They are made of less durable materials
questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: When comparing twentieth-century stadiums to ancient amphitheatres in Section D, which TWO negative features does the writer mention?
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“negative features”, “twentieth-century stadiums”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn D: “...mono-functional sports arenas...”
“Many such stadiums are situated in suburban areas...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“mono-functional” = chỉ có một chức năng → less versatile
“situated in suburban areas” → vị trí kém thuận tiện hơn
=> Đáp án:
They are in less convenient locations.
They are less versatile.

options.label: They are in less convenient locations.
They are in less convenient locations.
They are made of less durable materials

questions.prompt: When comparing twentieth-century stadiums to ancient amphitheatres in Section D, which TWO negative features does the writer mention?
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“negative features”, “twentieth-century stadiums”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn D: “...mono-functional sports arenas...”
“Many such stadiums are situated in suburban areas...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“mono-functional” = chỉ có một chức năng → less versatile
“situated in suburban areas” → vị trí kém thuận tiện hơn
=> Đáp án:
They are in less convenient locations.
They are less versatile.

options.label: They are in less convenient locations.
They are made of less durable materials

Choose TWO letters, A-E.

Which TWO advantages of modern stadium design does the writer mention?

A offering improved amenities for the enjoyment of sports events
B bringing community life back into the city environment
C facilitating research into solar and wind energy solutions
D enabling local residents to reduce their consumption of electricity
E providing a suitable site for the installation of renewable power generators

bringing community life back into the city environment

questions.explanation:

“advantages of modern stadium design”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn E và F
Bước 3: Phân tích:
Đoạn E: “...opens the space up to families and a wider cross-section of society...”

parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 27–40

passage.text: To catch a king
Anna Keay reviews Charles Spencer’s book about the hunt for King Charles II during the English Civil War of the seventeenth century

Charles Spencer’s latest book, To Catch a King, tells us the story of the hunt for King Charles II in the six weeks after his resounding defeat at the Battle of Worcester in September 1651. And what a story it is. After his father was executed by the Parliamentarians in 1649, the young Charles II sacrificed one of the very principles his father had died for and did a deal with Scots, thereby accepting Presbyterianism* as the national religion in return for being crowned King of Scots. His arrival in Edinburgh prompted the English Parliamentary army to invade Scotland in a pre-emptive strike. This was followed by a Scottish invasion of England. The two sides finally faced one another at Worcester in the west of England in 1651. After being comprehensively defeated on the meadows outside the city by the Parliamentarian army, the 21-year-old king found himself the subject of a national manhunt, with a huge sum offered for his capture, through a series of heart-poundingly close escapes, to evade the Parliamentarians before seeking refuge in France. For the next nine years, the penniless and defeated Charles wandered around Europe with only a small group of loyal supporters.

Years later, after his restoration as king, the 50-year-old Charles II requested a meeting with the writer and diarist Samuel Pepys. His intention when asking Pepys to commit his story to paper was to ensure that this most extraordinary episode was never forgotten. Over two three-hour sittings, the king related to him in great detail his personal recollections of the six weeks he had spent as a fugitive. As the king and secretary settled down (a scene that is surely a gift for a future scriptwriter), Charles commenced his story: ‘After the battle was so absolutely lost as to be beyond hope of recovery, I began to think of the best way of saving myself. ’

One of the joys of Spencer’s book, a result not least of its use of Charles II’s own narrative as well as those of his supporters, is just how close the reader gets to the action. The day-by-day retelling of the fugitives’ doings provides delicious details: the cutting of the king’s long hair with agricultural shears, the use of walnut leaves to dye his pale skin, and the day Charles spent lying on a branch of the great oak tree in Boscobel Wood as the Parliamentary soldiers scoured the forest floor below. Spencer draws out both the humour – such as the preposterous refusal of Charles’s friend Henry Wilmot to adopt disguise on the grounds that it was beneath his dignity – and the emotional tension when the secret of the king’s presence was cautiously revealed to his supporters.

Charles’s adventures after losing the Battle of Worcester hide the uncomfortable truth that whilst almost everyone in England had been appalled by the execution of his father, they had not welcomed the arrival of his son with the Scots army, but had instead firmly bolted their doors. This was partly because he rode at the head of what looked like a foreign invasion force and partly because, after almost a decade of civil war, people were desperate to avoid it beginning again. This makes it all the more interesting that Charles II himself loved the story so much ever after. As well as retelling it to anyone who would listen, causing eye-rolling among courtiers, he set in train a series of initiatives to memorialise it. There was to be a new order of chivalry, the Knights of the Royal Oak. A series of enormous oil paintings depicting the episode were produced, including a two-metre-wide canvas of Boscobel Wood and a set of six similarly enormous paintings of the king on the run. In 1660, Charles II commissioned the artist John Michael Wright to paint a flying squadron of cherubs* carrying an oak tree to the heavens on the ceiling of his bedchamber. It is hard to imagine many other kings marking the lowest point in their life so enthusiastically, or indeed pulling off such an escape in the first place.

Charles Spencer is the perfect person to pass the story on to a new generation. His pacey, readable prose steers deftly clear of modern idioms and elegantly brings to life the details of the great tale. He has even-handed sympathy for both the fugitive king and the fierce republican regime that hunted him, and he succeeds in his desire to explore far more of the background of the story than previous books on the subject have done. Indeed, the opening third of the book is about how Charles II found himself at Worcester in the first place, which for some will be reason alone to read To Catch a King.

The tantalizing question left, in the end, is that of what it all meant. Would Charles II have been a different king had these six weeks never happened? The days and nights spent in hiding must have affected him in some way. Did the need to assume disguises, to survive on wit and charm alone, to use trickery and subterfuge to escape from tight corners help form him? This is the one area where the book doesn’t quite hit the mark. Instead its depiction of Charles II in his final years as an ineffective, pleasure-loving monarch doesn’t do justice to the man (neither is it accurate), or to the complexity of his character. But this one niggle aside, To Catch a King is an excellent read, and those who come to it knowing little of the famous tale will find they have a treat in store.
Presbyterianism: part of the reformed Protestant religion
cherub: an image of angelic children used in paintings

parts.passageHtml:
To catch a king

Anna Keay reviews Charles Spencer’s book about the hunt for King Charles II during the English Civil War of the seventeenth century

Charles Spencer’s latest book, To Catch a King, tells us the story of the hunt for King Charles II in the six weeks after his resounding defeat at the Battle of Worcester in September 1651. And what a story it is. His arrival in Edinburgh prompted the English Parliamentary army to invade Scotland in a pre-emptive strike. This was followed by a Scottish invasion of England. The two sides finally faced one another at Worcester in the west of England in 1651. After being comprehensively defeated on the meadows outside the city by the Parliamentarian army, the 21-year-old king found himself the subject of a national manhunt, with a huge sum offered for his capture, through a series of heart-poundingly close escapes, to evade the Parliamentarians before seeking refuge in France. For the next nine years, the penniless and defeated Charles wandered around Europe with only a small group of loyal supporters.

His intention when asking Pepys to commit his story to paper was to ensure that this most extraordinary episode was never forgotten. As well as retelling it to anyone who would listen, causing eye-rolling among courtiers, he set in train a series of initiatives to memorialise it. A series of enormous oil paintings depicting the episode were produced, including a two-metre-wide canvas of Boscobel Wood and a set of six similarly enormous paintings of the king on the run. His pacey, readable prose steers deftly clear of modern idioms and elegantly brings to life the details of the great tale. He has even-handed sympathy for both the fugitive king and the fierce republican regime that hunted him, and he succeeds in his desire to explore far more of the background of the story than previous books on the subject have done. Indeed, the opening third of the book is about how Charles II found himself at Worcester in the first place, which for some will be reason alone to read To Catch a King.

The tantalizing question left, in the end, is that of what it all meant. Would Charles II have been a different king had these six weeks never happened? The days and nights spent in hiding must have affected him in some way. Did the need to assume disguises, to survive on wit and charm alone, to use trickery and subterfuge to escape from tight corners help form him? This is the one area where the book doesn’t quite hit the mark. Instead its depiction of Charles II in his final years as an ineffective, pleasure-loving monarch doesn’t do justice to the man (neither is it accurate), or to the complexity of his character. But this one niggle aside, To Catch a King is an excellent read, and those who come to it knowing little of the famous tale will find they have a treat in store.

  • Presbyterianism: part of the reformed Protestant religion

  • cherub: an image of angelic children used in paintings

questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 27-31

Complete the summary using the list of phrases, A-J, below.

A military innovation B large reward C widespread conspiracy D relative safety E new government F decisive victory G political debate H strategic alliance I popular solution J religious conviction
questionGroups.instruction: Questions 27-31Complete the summary using the list of phrases, A-J, below.A military innovation B large reward C widespread conspiracy D relative safety E new government F decisive victory G political debate H strategic alliance I popular solution J religious conviction
questionGroups.blockTitle: The story behind the hunt for Charles II
questionGroups.blockHtml:
The story behind the hunt for Charles II

Charles II’s father was executed by the Parliamentarian forces in 1649. Charles II then formed a 27_______ with the Scots, and in order to become King of Scots, he abandoned an important 28_______ that was held by his father and had contributed to his father’s death. The opposing sides then met outside Worcester in 1651. The battle led to a 29_______ for the Parliamentarians and Charles had to flee for his life. A 30_______ was offered for Charles’s capture, but after six weeks spent in hiding, he eventually managed to reach the 31_______ of continental Europe.
questionGroups.noteContent: Charles II’s father was executed by the Parliamentarian forces in 1649. Charles II then formed a [27] with the Scots, and in order to become King of Scots, he abandoned an important [28] that was held by his father and had contributed to his father’s death. The opposing sides then met outside Worcester in 1651. The battle led to a [29] for the Parliamentarians and Charles had to flee for his life. A [30] was offered for Charles’s capture, but after six weeks spent in hiding, he eventually managed to reach the [31] of continental Europe.

questions.prompt: Charles II then formed a 27 ………………… with the Scots
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“formed a … with the Scots”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 1: “...the young Charles II ... did a deal with Scots...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“did a deal with” = formed an alliance
Trong đáp án, cụm phù hợp nhất là strategic alliance
=> Đáp án: strategic alliance
questions.questionHtml:
The story behind the hunt for Charles II

Charles II’s father was executed by the Parliamentarian forces in 1649. Charles II then formed a 27_______ with the Scots, and in order to become King of Scots, he abandoned an important 28_______ that was held by his father and had contributed to his father’s death. The opposing sides then met outside Worcester in 1651. The battle led to a 29_______ for the Parliamentarians and Charles had to flee for his life. A 30_______ was offered for Charles’s capture, but after six weeks spent in hiding, he eventually managed to reach the 31_______ of continental Europe.

questions.prompt: and in order to become King of Scots, he abandoned an important 28 ………………… that was held by his father
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“abandoned an important … held by his father”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 1: “...sacrificed one of the very principles his father had died for and did a deal with Scots, thereby accepting Presbyterianism...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“principles” liên quan đến niềm tin tôn giáo của cha ông
Đáp án phù hợp nhất là religious conviction
=> Đáp án: religious conviction

questions.prompt: The battle led to a 29 ………………… for the Parliamentarians
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword: 
“The battle led to a … for the Parliamentarians”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 1: “After being comprehensively defeated... by the Parliamentarian army...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“comprehensively defeated” = bị đánh bại hoàn toàn
→ Parliamentarians giành chiến thắng lớn
=> Đáp án: decisive victory

questions.prompt: A 30 ………………… was offered for Charles’s capture
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“offered for Charles’s capture”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 1:“...with a huge sum offered for his capture...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“a huge sum” = large reward
=> Đáp án: large reward

questions.prompt: but after six weeks spent in hiding, he eventually managed to reach the 31 ………………… of continental Europe.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“managed to reach the … of continental Europe”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 1: “...before seeking refuge in France.”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“seeking refuge” = tìm nơi an toàn
Đáp án phù hợp là relative safety
=> Đáp án: relative safety

questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 32-35

Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer?
questionGroups.instruction: Questions 32-35Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer?
questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: Charles chose Pepys for the task because he considered him to be trustworthy.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“Pepys”, “trustworthy”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 2 chỉ nói: “Charles II requested a meeting with the writer and diarist Samuel Pepys.”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
Bài không nói Charles chọn Pepys vì đáng tin cậy
Không đủ thông tin
=> Đáp án: NOT GIVEN

questions.prompt: Charles’s personal recollection of the escape lacked sufficient detail.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“lacked sufficient detail”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 2: “...the king related to him in great detail his personal recollections...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“in great detail” = rất chi tiết
Statement nói thiếu chi tiết → trái ngược bài
=> Đáp án: NO

questions.prompt: Charles indicated to Pepys that he had planned his escape before the battle.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“planned his escape before the battle”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 2: “After the battle was so absolutely lost ... I began to think of the best way of saving myself.”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
Charles chỉ nghĩ đến việc trốn thoát sau khi trận chiến thất bại
Không phải trước trận chiến
=> Đáp án: NO

questions.prompt: The inclusion of Charles’s account is a positive aspect of the book.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“positive aspect of the book”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 3: “One of the joys of Spencer’s book ... is just how close the reader gets to the action.”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
Reviewer đánh giá cao việc dùng lời kể của Charles II
→ đây là điểm tích cực của cuốn sách
=> Đáp án: YES

questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 36-40

Choose the correct letter, A, B, C, or D.
questionGroups.instruction: Questions 36-40Choose the correct letter, A, B, C, or D.
questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: What is the reviewer’s main purpose in the first paragraph?
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“main purpose in the first paragraph”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 1 nói về: Charles II liên minh với Scots
trận Worcester
bị truy đuổi và trốn sang Pháp
Bước 3: Phân tích:
Đoạn này chủ yếu giải thích hoàn cảnh dẫn đến cuộc trốn chạy của Charles II
=> Đáp án: B
to give an account of the circumstances leading to Charles II’s escape

options.label: to describe what happened during the Battle of Worcester
to give an account of the circumstances leading to Charles II’s escape
to provide details of the Parliamentarians’ political views
to compare Charles II’s beliefs with those of his father

questions.prompt: Why does the reviewer include examples of the fugitives’ behaviour in the third paragraph?
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“examples of the fugitives’ behaviour”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 3: “The day-by-day retelling ... provides delicious details...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
Các ví dụ được đưa ra để làm câu chuyện sống động và chân thực hơn
=> Đáp án: C
to illustrate how the events of the six weeks are brought to life

options.label: to explain how close Charles II came to losing his life
to give an accouto suggest that Charles II’s supporters were badly preparednt of the circumstances leading to Charles II’s escape
to illustrate how the events of the six weeks are brought to life
to argue that certain aspects are not as well known as they should be

questions.prompt: What point does the reviewer make about Charles II in the fourth paragraph?
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“Charles II in the fourth paragraph”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 4: “It is hard to imagine many other kings marking the lowest point in their life so enthusiastically...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
Charles II lại nhiệt tình kỷ niệm giai đoạn thất bại nhất đời mình
=> Đáp án: A
He chose to celebrate what was essentially a defeat.

options.label: He chose to celebrate what was essentially a defeat.
He misunderstood the motives of his opponents.
He aimed to restore people’s faith in the monarchy.
He was driven by a desire to be popular.

questions.prompt: 39 What does the reviewer say about Charles Spencer in the fifth paragraph?
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“Charles Spencer”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn 5: “He has even-handed sympathy for both the fugitive king and the fierce republican regime...”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
“even-handed sympathy” = thái độ công bằng, không thiên vị
=> Đáp án: B
He takes an unbiased approach to the subject matter.

options.label: His decision to write the book comes as a surprise.
He takes an unbiased approach to the subject matter.
His descriptions of events would be better if they included more detail.
He chooses language that is suitable for a twenty-first-century audience.

questions.prompt: 40 When the reviewer says the book ‘doesn’t quite hit the mark’, she is making the point that
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword:
“doesn’t quite hit the mark”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn:
Đoạn cuối: “Would Charles II have been a different king had these six weeks never happened?”
“This is the one area where the book doesn’t quite hit the mark.”
Bước 3: Phân tích:
Reviewer cho rằng sách chưa giải thích rõ liệu trải nghiệm đó có ảnh hưởng lâu dài đến Charles II hay không
=> Đáp án: D
it fails to address whether Charles II’s experiences had a lasting influence on him.

options.label: it overlooks the impact of events on ordinary people.
it lacks an analysis of prevalent views on monarchy.
it omits any references to the deceit practised by Charles II during his time in hiding.
it fails to address whether Charles II’s experiences had a lasting influence on him.
